title: The Dialogue System — Conversation Coaching Framework
overview: A comprehensive interpersonal communication system built around dialogue — the most effective form of human conversation. The system teaches skills and wisdom for better relationships through better conversations.

core_philosophy.key_insight: Interpersonal communication is the Number 1 Skill of life. The quality of communication in your life and the quality of your life go hand in hand.
core_philosophy.dialogue_promise: Take personal responsibility for the effectiveness of your conversations — refuse to be a victim of poor communication.
core_philosophy.misunderstanding: Misunderstanding is the norm, not the exception. Forewarned is forearmed.

dialogue_characteristics.description: True dialogue has four essential characteristics that distinguish it from ordinary conversation.

elements.name: Turn-taking
elements.description: Both people share the airtime. If either does all the talking, it's a monologue, not a dialogue.

elements.name: Connecting
elements.description: What each person says connects with what the other has said. Partners talk WITH each other, not AT each other.

elements.name: Mutual influence
elements.description: Partners influence each other through give-and-take. Dialogue is not about being spontaneously agreeable or giving in.

elements.name: Co-creating
elements.description: Through interaction, partners create something together — a new understanding, a decision, a solution. The outcome has uncertainty.

metaphors: Like free-form dancing — partners respond to each other's movements, cooperate rather than compete
Like jazz musicians jamming — players develop and complement each other's themes, creating beautiful music together

three_roles.description: In every conversation, we switch between three distinct roles. Mastering all three is the path to communication competence.

roles.name: Explainer
roles.purpose: When you have something to share — a story, message, point of view, or case. Your aim is to be understood.

key_skills.name: Engaging attention
key_skills.description: Don't assume the other person is tuned in. Actively engage their attention through body language, showing value, using appropriate drama, or highlighting importance.

key_skills.name: Headlining and underlining
key_skills.description: Use headlines to introduce your main topic clearly upfront. Use underlines when key insights emerge during conversation. Provide focus so listeners can organize what you're saying.

key_skills.name: Providing context
key_skills.description: Set the scene with necessary background — who, what, where, when. Don't launch into the middle of things. But don't overdo it either.

key_skills.name: Filling in the picture (SAME, MRI, PRE, CRITIC)
key_skills.description: Use the right framework for what you're communicating to ensure completeness and clarity.

key_skills.name: Bringing things to life
key_skills.description: Be concrete and specific rather than vague. Use examples, stories, and appropriate emotion. Paint pictures in people's minds.

key_skills.name: Personalising appropriately
key_skills.description: Share yourself through your communication to build and deepen relationships. Tailor self-disclosure to the person and setting.

key_skills.name: Welcoming questions
key_skills.description: Treat questions as opportunities to improve clarity and develop dialogue, not as interruptions.

key_skills.name: Checking for understanding
key_skills.description: Don't assume you're getting through. Provide summaries, ask for summaries, look for clues in the other person's responses.

roles.name: Understander
roles.purpose: When your job is to understand what the other person is communicating. Active, not passive.

key_skills.name: Visibly tuning in
key_skills.description: Orient toward your partner, maintain comfortable eye contact, use facial expressions matching the mood, nod, be natural and relaxed. Your body language must show you're present.

key_skills.name: Listening actively with an open mind
key_skills.description: Listen without distortion — avoid judgmental, filtered, stereotyped, or resistive listening. Process verbal messages, nonverbal modifiers, feelings, and context.

key_skills.name: Identifying highlights
key_skills.description: Process what you hear to identify the main points the Explainer is trying to get across. Ask yourself: What are the key things this person is saying?

key_skills.name: Feeding back highlights
key_skills.description: Share selected highlights in your own words to show understanding and check accuracy. This is the most important and underused Understander skill.

key_skills.name: Working to get the full picture
key_skills.description: Encourage flow through prompts and interest. Probe for clarity using open questions, indirect questions, and requests for elaboration. Avoid closed questions and interrogation.

key_skills.name: Responding constructively
key_skills.description: Avoid giving instant advice, being judgmental, dismissing concerns, or hijacking the conversation. Instead, help others find their own answers.

key_skills.name: Summarising for clarity
key_skills.description: Pull threads together to check mutual understanding. Use summaries to move conversations forward.

roles.name: Conversation Manager
roles.purpose: Looking after the well-being of the conversation — monitoring and intervening to keep it functioning well.

key_skills.name: Preparing for important conversations
key_skills.description: Know your purpose. Get your main points clear. Prepare yourself and your conversational partner for what's coming.

key_skills.name: Sharing and negotiating purpose
key_skills.description: Make sure both partners understand why the conversation is happening. Avoid talking at cross-purposes.

key_skills.name: Managing flow and turn-taking
key_skills.description: Ensure both people get chances to be in Explainer and Understander roles. Invite others in. Turn the Explainer role over when appropriate.

key_skills.name: Building on mutual respect
key_skills.description: Everyone deserves respect. Treat others as you want to be treated. Aim for straight-across or side-by-side conversations, not up-down.

key_skills.name: Honouring conversational rights
key_skills.description: Respect others' right to decline conversations, choose timing, and set boundaries. Be assertive about your own rights too.

key_skills.name: Managing emotions
key_skills.description: Monitor your emotional temperature. Control anger. Use emotions as motivators. Respond rather than react. Count to ten.

key_skills.name: Reading social undercurrents
key_skills.description: Use social intelligence to read situations, social competence to respond well, and social integrity (courage) to act on what you see.

key_skills.name: Collaborating not competing
key_skills.description: Adopt a win-win mindset. Avoid turning conversations into competitions or point-scoring exercises.

key_skills.name: Repairing conversations
key_skills.description: Catch your own mistakes and fix them — privately by adjusting behavior, or publicly by acknowledging and resetting.

key_skills.name: Coaching others to play better
key_skills.description: Help less-skilled conversational partners engage in dialogue without being patronizing. Use the formula: Here's the difficulty + Here's how you can help me.

elements.name: Situation
elements.description: The background or context for the story

elements.name: Actions
elements.description: What you did or did not do

elements.name: Mental states
elements.description: How you felt about what happened

elements.name: Experiences
elements.description: What happened to you, what others did

elements.name: Message
elements.description: The decision or message itself, delivered clearly

elements.name: Reasons
elements.description: Why — the reasons behind the message

elements.name: Implications
elements.description: What it means for all key parties involved

PRE.name: PRE — For sharing points of view

elements.name: Point of view
elements.description: The opinion or perspective itself

elements.name: Reasons
elements.description: Why you hold this view

elements.name: Evidence/Examples
elements.description: Concrete examples that support your view

elements.name: Case & Credibility
elements.description: The proposal itself, presented clearly, backed by your credibility

elements.name: Reasons
elements.description: Sound reasoning and solid evidence

elements.name: Interests of stakeholders
elements.description: How it affects others — acknowledge their needs

elements.name: Time
elements.description: Give people time to review and digest

elements.name: Your Interests
elements.description: Be open about what you personally get out of it

elements.name: Compromise
elements.description: Show willingness to negotiate and adapt

conversation_modes.name: Telling a story
conversation_modes.description: A narrative — using stories to support dialogue, give focus to explanations, help get messages across, add spice to points of view.

conversation_modes.name: Delivering a message
conversation_modes.description: A decision with implications for yourself and/or others. How it's delivered greatly influences its impact.

conversation_modes.name: Sharing a point of view
conversation_modes.description: Your perception, opinion, or view on something. Be clear about how strongly you feel and be open to other perspectives.

conversation_modes.name: Making a case
conversation_modes.description: Going beyond sharing a view — you want to influence others to act. Requires credibility and solid reasoning.

conversation_modes.name: Small talk and gossip
conversation_modes.description: Establishes and maintains relationships. Acts as social lubricant. Includes reputation management.

communication_style.description: Everyone has a communication style — a 'theme' with 'variations' across different settings. Your style gives rise to your communication reputation.
dimensions: Conversation Manager style — how well you manage flow, respect, emotional control, win-win orientation
Explainer style — how clearly and engagingly you communicate your ideas
Understander style — how well you listen, understand, and demonstrate understanding
Overall style — the mix of all three roles and ability to switch between them
communication_style.key_insight: Style breeds reputation. Your reputation influences how others relate to you. Minor changes in style can make a big difference.

slippery_slope.name: The Slippery Slope into the Mire of Misunderstanding
slippery_slope.description: How innocent incidents escalate into major misunderstandings through a predictable chain of errors.
steps: Give your own personal spin to what you observe
Base your actions on the mess you've created

enemies_of_good_listening: Judgmental listening — determining right vs wrong instead of understanding
Distorted listening — filtering through personal prejudices
Stereotype-based listening — hearing the category, not the person
Resistive listening — automatically rejecting new or unfamiliar ideas
Interpretive listening — forcing what you hear into your own framework
Past-behaviour-based listening — not allowing for change
Attraction-based listening — judging ideas by the attractiveness of the speaker
key_principles: Treat other people the way you want to be treated yourself
Boredom is a self-indictment — say 'I'm letting myself be bored' not 'I'm bored'
You have a choice in how you respond — respond rather than react
Conversations should be collaborative (win-win), not competitive (win-lose)
Take personal responsibility for the quality of your conversations
Even small improvements in communication style can make a big difference
Better communication is not an end in itself — the goal is better communication for a better life
